locked_facts: In a uniform field, V = -E·d (potential decreases in the direction of E)
Equipotential surfaces are perpendicular to electric field lines
Work done by field: W = q(V_A - V_B) = qE·d
E = -dV/dr (field is negative gradient of potential)
No work is done in moving a charge along an equipotential surface
Electric field points from high potential to low potential
Potential due to point charge: V = kq/r (scalar, not vector)
truth_statements: The electric field always points in the direction of decreasing potential
Equipotential surfaces are always perpendicular to the electric field at every point
The work done in moving a charge between two points depends only on the potential difference and the charge, not on the path
In a uniform field the equipotential surfaces are equally spaced parallel planes
A stronger electric field is represented by more closely spaced equipotential surfaces

aha_moment.what_to_show: Parallel equipotential lines with E arrows pointing from high V to low V — a positive charge placed between them accelerates downhill from high to low potential, like a ball rolling downhill on a potential slope
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: E arrows pointing from the high-V surface to the low-V surface, with a charge rolling 'downhill'
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students confuse high field with high potential — seeing E as the slope of the potential landscape clarifies that strong E means steep potential change

simulation_states.label: Uniform field setup
simulation_states.description: Parallel plate capacitor with uniform E field between plates. Field arrows shown. Potential labels: V_high on left plate, V_low on right plate.
simulation_states.emphasis: E field is uniform between parallel plates

simulation_states.label: Equipotential lines
simulation_states.description: Vertical equipotential lines appear between plates, equally spaced. Labels show voltage values decreasing from left to right. E arrows perpendicular to each equipotential.
simulation_states.emphasis: Equipotentials are perpendicular to E and equally spaced in uniform field

simulation_states.label: Charge moves downhill
simulation_states.description: A positive test charge placed at high-V side accelerates toward low-V side along E. Energy bar shows PE converting to KE. The 'potential hill' metaphor is visualised as a slope.
simulation_states.emphasis: E points from HIGH to LOW potential — the charge rolls downhill

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student adjusts voltage difference and plate separation. Equipotential spacing changes (closer = stronger E). Moving charge along equipotential shows zero work.
simulation_states.emphasis: Closer equipotentials = stronger field. Moving along an equipotential = zero work.

common_misconceptions.belief: High electric field means high potential at that point
common_misconceptions.correction: Electric field is the rate of change of potential — a region can have strong E but any value of V. E = -dV/dr means strong field = rapid change in potential
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show a region with strong E where V is actually low and decreasing

common_misconceptions.belief: Work done depends on the path taken between two points
common_misconceptions.correction: Electrostatic force is conservative — work depends only on the potential difference between start and end points, not the path
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show two different paths between same points — same W = qΔV for both
